text:
May it please the court: my client, the slice of chocolate cake, stands accused of arriving before the entrée, in flagrant violation of the so-called natural order of meals. I intend to prove this order is a fraud perpetrated by Big Vegetable.

Consider, first, the doctrine of mortality. We are all, at every moment, one rogue bus away from never finishing our meal. To eat the broccoli first and die before the tiramisu is the single greatest tragedy a human can suffer, far worse than anything in the news. Dessert first is not gluttony; it is prudent risk management. I'd call it an insurance policy you get to eat.

Second, the science. Stomachs operate on a first-come, first-served basis with no reserved seating. By the time you've trudged through a chicken breast and a heap of rice, the prime real estate is gone, and the cheesecake is forced to stand in the aisle. Eat it first and it gets the good seat, the leg room, the full attention of your taste buds while they're still fresh and unjaded by salad.

Third, and most damning to the prosecution: children are made to eat vegetables before dessert specifically as a punishment and a bribe. You, an adult, with a job and a mortgage and the legal right to operate a motor vehicle, are no longer subject to this regime. To eat your peas before your pie is to voluntarily remain a hostage in a negotiation you already won years ago. It's Stockholm syndrome with gravy.

The defense rests, ideally on a full stomach of pie. I move for immediate acquittal and a second helping.
